extra.message: User-controlled `username` from POST body is directly concatenated into an SQL query via f-string in `cursor.execute()`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by providing a malicious `username` value (e.g. `' OR 1=1--`), bypassing authentication and extracting all records.

User-controlled `mathexp` from POST body is concatenated directly into a Jinja2 Template string (line 10) and then rendered (line 12). An attacker can inject arbitrary Jinja2 template expressions, leading to SSTI and potential remote code execution (e.g. `{{ ''.__class__.__mro__[2].__subclasses__() }}`).

User-controlled `filename` from POST body (line 13) is passed directly to `open()` (line 16 via `fetchfile()` which calls `open()` on line 3). No path sanitization is performed, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files on the server (e.g. `../../etc/passwd`).

User-controlled `imagelink` from POST body (line 15) is passed directly to `requests.get()` (line 5 in `fetchimage()`). An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/ for cloud metadata, or internal network services), enabling SSRF attacks.

`verify=False` is passed to `requests.get()` disabling SSL/TLS certificate validation. This makes the application vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks, allowing an attacker to intercept and modify the response from any HTTPS URL the server fetches.

The `Host` header from the incoming request (line 12) is reflected unsanitized into an HTML anchor tag's `href` attribute (line 18). An attacker can control the Host header to inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g. sending `Host: example.com'><script>alert(1)</script>` to achieve XSS), or use this for open redirect and cache poisoning.

User-controlled `username` from POST body (line 7) is reflected unsanitized in the JSON response (line 10). While JSON responses mitigate direct browser XSS, any client-side consumer that embeds this value into HTML without escaping is vulnerable. Additionally, if Content-Type negotiation is misconfigured, this could lead to reflected XSS.

Database exception messages are returned directly to the client via `{"msg": f"{e}"}`. This leaks internal database error details (table structure, error types) that can assist an attacker in crafting more precise SQL injection payloads.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through Error Messages

extra.message: Hardcoded credentials are inserted into the USERS table in plaintext: `mike/kaines` (line 25) and `admin/admin` (line 28). These credentials are hardcoded in source code and can be discovered by anyone with access to the repository.

Flask application runs with `debug=True` bound to `0.0.0.0:8000`. This enables the Werkzeug debugger and allows arbitrary code execution via the debugger console. Binding to `0.0.0.0` makes it accessible to any network interface.

cwe: CWE-215: Information Exposure Through Debug Information
